{{日本の氏族
|家名 = Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko
|家紋 =
|家紋名称 =
|本姓 = {{ill|Hasebe clan|ja|丈部氏}} ({{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}})<br />{{ill|Mibu clan|ja|壬生氏}} ({{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}} of {{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}})
|家祖 = {{ill|Aheshiki-no-Mikoto|ja|阿閇色命}}<ref>''Sendai Kuji Hongi'' Kokuzō Hongi</ref><br />Tsukutan-no-Mikoto<ref>''Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki''</ref>
|種別 = {{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別}}
|出身地 =
|根拠地 = Tsukuba Province<br />(later {{ill|Tsukuba District|ja|筑波郡}} of {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}})
|人物 = See [[#People|People]]
|支流 = {{ill|Mibu clan|ja|壬生氏}}
}}

'''Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''tsukuba no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''tsukuba kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled the southern part of {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}.

== Overview ==
=== Ancestry ===
*According to the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}", during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇|lt=Emperor Seimu|lt_ja=成務朝}}, '''Aheshiki-no-Mikoto''', grandson of Oshikorimi-no-Mikoto, was first appointed Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
*According to the ''{{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', the Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko '''{{ill|Tsukutan-no-Mikoto|ja|筑箪命}}''' (of the same lineage as the {{ill|Uneme clan|ja|采女氏}}), who changed the name of this area — formerly called "Ki no Kuni" — to "Tsukuba" after his own name, is described as a kuni no miyatsuko of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|lt=Emperor Sujin|lt_ja=崇神朝}} court before the Seimu court.

=== Clan ===
In the ''{{ill|Shōsōin Monjo|ja|正倉院文書}}'', the name Hasebe-no-Atai-Samimaro appears as deputy vice-administrator of {{ill|Tsukuba District|ja|筑波郡}} — who can be regarded as a descendant of the Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko — and so there is a theory that it was the '''Hasebe-no-Atai clan'''<ref>Saeki Arikiyo, "Fundamental Research on the Hasebe Clan and Hasebe," in Saeki Arikiyo (ed.), ''Nihon Kodai-shi Ronkō'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980).</ref>.

Also, {{ill|Mibu-no-Atai-Oyakanushime|ja|壬生小家主女|lt=Mibu-no-Atai-Oyakanushime|lt_ja=壬生直小家主女}}, an {{ill|uneme|ja|采女}} from {{ill|Tsukuba District|ja|筑波郡}} in {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}} who served {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}} (Empress Shōtoku) as {{ill|Shōzen|ja|膳部|lt=Shōzen|lt_ja=尚膳}} and was appointed Hitachi {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} in {{ill|Jingo-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 2 ({{ill|768|ja|768年}}) Month 6<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'' Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku Jingo-keiun Year 2 Month 6 Day 14 entry.</ref>, is considered to be a descendant of the Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko, and therefore the '''Mibu-no-Atai clan''' is also said to have been a clan of the Tsukuba no Kuni no Miyatsuko<ref>Tsukuba-chō Shi Editorial Committee (ed.), ''Tsukuba-chō Shi'' (Tsukuba City, 1990).</ref>.

== Base ==
'''Tsukuba-gō''' in {{ill|Tsukuba District|ja|筑波郡}}.

=== Territory ===
The territory of the kuni no miyatsuko was the region then called '''Tsukuba Province''', corresponding to the later Tsukuba District of {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}, equivalent to part of present-day {{ill|Tsuchiura, Ibaraki|ja|土浦市|lt=Tsuchiura|lt_ja=土浦市}} and most of {{ill|Tsukuba, Ibaraki|ja|つくば市|lt=Tsukuba|lt_ja=つくば市}} and {{ill|Tsukubamirai, Ibaraki|ja|つくばみらい市|lt=Tsukubamirai|lt_ja=つくばみらい市}} in {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県}}. According to the ''{{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Tsukuba's old name was "Ki no Kuni" (i.e., {{ill|Kenu|ja|毛野}} Province), and the west of Tsukuba is connected with the Kenu River (Kinugawa River), and it is close to Shimotsukenu; so it has been pointed out that Kenu at a certain period may have been a region encompassing not only the Nasu region but also the area around {{ill|Niihari District|ja|新治郡|lt=Niihari|lt_ja=新治}}, {{ill|Makabe District|ja|真壁郡|lt=Shirakabe|lt_ja=白壁}}, and Tsukuba of {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国}}. From the latter half of the 4th century to the beginning of the 5th century, keyhole-shaped kofun in this area are distributed around the Ogawa and {{ill|Yuzukami Village|ja|湯津上村|lt=Yuzukami|lt_ja=湯津上}} areas of Tochigi Prefecture and the Tsukuba and Kakioka areas of Ibaraki Prefecture; the Maruyama Kofun in {{ill|Ishioka, Ibaraki|ja|石岡市}} of Ibaraki Prefecture and the Nasu-Hachimanzuka Kofun in {{ill|Nakagawa, Tochigi|ja|那珂川町}} of {{ill|Nasu District|ja|那須郡}} in Tochigi Prefecture agree even in mound form and internal structure<ref>Shida Jun'ichi, "The Source of Eastern Japan's Underlying Strength," in Mori Kōichi (ed.), ''Nihon no Kodai'' Vol. 2: ''Retto no Chiiki Bunka'' (Chūō Kōron-sha, 1986-02-20).</ref>.

== Tutelary Shrine ==
The only {{ill|Engishiki-listed shrine|ja|式内社}} existing in {{ill|Tsukuba District|ja|筑波郡}} — '''{{ill|Tsukubasan Shrine|ja|筑波山神社}}'''. It enshrines Tsukuba-Ogami and Tsukuba-Megami.

=== Tombs ===
*'''Kisaki-zuka Kofun'''
*:A 53.8-meter-long {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun (rectangle)|ja|前方後方墳}} in {{ill|Tsuchiura, Ibaraki|ja|土浦市}} in {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県}}, built in the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}.
*'''Ōzuka Kofun'''
*:An 84-meter-long handle-mirror-style {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in Tsuchiura City, Ibaraki Prefecture, built in the early Kofun period. Built almost adjacent to the southeast of Kisaki-zuka Kofun, it was assumed to be the next generation after Kisaki-zuka Kofun, but in recent years there is also dissent<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=石田温美, 荒井啓汰, 齊木誠 |title=土浦市后塚古墳の測量調査 |journal=筑波大学先史学・考古学研究 |issn=0918-0419 |publisher=筑波大学人文社会科学研究科歴史・人類学専攻 |year=2018 |issue=29 |pages=69-83(p.69) |naid=120006622169 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2241/00155248}}</ref>.
*'''{{ill|Tsukuba-Hachimanzuka Kofun|ja|筑波八幡塚古墳}}'''
*:A 90-meter-long keyhole-shaped kofun in {{ill|Tsukuba, Ibaraki|ja|つくば市}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, built in the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}. Though the construction period differs from the tradition of being the tomb of Aheshiki-no-Mikoto, it is regarded as the tomb of one who became kuni no miyatsuko<ref name="houga">{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男}}, "[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/hitori/takaosan2.htm Kofun with Shrines on Their Mounds]," ''Koju Kinobōma'', 2015.</ref>.

== Footnotes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== Citations ===
{{Reflist}}

== References ==
* ''Kokushi Daijiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan)
* Sakamoto Tarō, Hirano Kunio, ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1990)
* ''Nihon-shi Kō-jiten'' (Yamakawa Shuppansha)
* ''Shintō Daijiten'' (Rinsen Shoten)

== See also ==
* {{ill|List of Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|国造の一覧}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:つくはのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:日本の歴史関連のスタブ項目]]
[[Category:常陸国]]
[[Category:茨城県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:筑波国造]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
